text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 6 May 2025 

Dr Patel  
St George's University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Department of Neurology, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Roberts, DOB: 14-02-1989, Hospital No: H927451 NHS No. 8472395610  
Moorings 12, Regent’s Canal, London, NW1 8AL  

Diagnoses: 

Recurrent unprovoked seizures; aetiology and electroclinical classification currently unclear. 

Thank you for asking me to review Michael Roberts in clinic today. The history below is patient-reported. He lives on a houseboat/mobile home and reports occasional power outages that intermittently affect charging of his wearable seizure-alert device and phone. He is not currently using a VNS or other implanted neuromodulation. There have been no recent hospital admissions for seizures. No new injuries reported. 

Seizure history and current pattern: 
- Seizures in 2024-2025: Aug: 6 days, most of them at sleep time, Sep: 11 days with seizures, Oct: 1 days, Nov: 7 days, most of them at sleep time, Dec: 8 days with seizures Jan: 9 days, Feb: 8 days with seizures Mar: 2 days. 

He describes events as sudden loss of awareness with stiffening followed by shaking in some episodes, and in others a brief behavioural arrest with blank staring; occasional tongue biting noted on waking. Post-event fatigue can last several hours. He estimates most events in August and November occurred during sleep. He does not drive. 

Medications: 
- Lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily (self-reported; adherence occasionally impacted when charging reminders fail during power cuts). 
- Buccal midazolam is not held; no recent emergency rescue therapy used. 

Investigations and monitoring: 
- Prior routine EEG (2023) reportedly normal per patient; formal report not available to review today. 
- No recent MRI brain available in our system; patient believes an MRI was performed elsewhere several years ago. 
- No device diagnostics applicable as he does not have an implanted system. 

Assessment: 
The reported frequency shows variability month to month with several nocturnal events. History is patient-reported and partially limited by lack of contemporaneous records and intermittent disruption of digital seizure logging due to power outages on the houseboat. There is no clear precipitant identified. Further classification remains pending correlation with investigations. 

Plan agreed today: 
- Request prior EEG and MRI reports from external providers; if not obtainable, arrange repeat MRI brain (epilepsy protocol) and sleep-deprived EEG. 
- Provide a paper seizure diary as backup when devices cannot be charged; reinforce timestamping of events and any potential triggers. 
- Check lamotrigine trough level and routine bloods (U&E, LFT, FBC). 
- Discussed SUDEP information and nocturnal seizure safety; provided written advice. 
- Community epilepsy nurse to contact patient regarding strategies for medication adherence during power outages. 
- Follow-up in 3 months or sooner if events escalate, injuries occur, or prolonged episodes develop. 

Yours sincerely

Seizures in 2024-2025: Aug: 6 days, most of them at sleep time, Sep: 11 days with seizures, Oct: 1 days, Nov: 7 days, most of them at sleep time, Dec: 8 days with seizures Jan: 9 days, Feb: 8 days with seizures Mar: 2 days

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides monthly counts of days with seizures from Aug 2024 to Mar 2025: Aug 6, Sep 11, Oct 1, Nov 7, Dec 8, Jan 9, Feb 8, Mar 2. Summing these: 6 + 11 + 1 + 7 + 8 + 9 + 8 + 2 = 52 seizure days. The observation window spans 8 months (Aug 2024 through Mar 2025). Therefore, the frequency can be expressed as 52 per 8 month.
seizure_frequency_number: Seizures in 2024-2025: Aug: 6 days, most of them at sleep time, Sep: 11 days with seizures, Oct: 1 days, Nov: 7 days, most of them at sleep time, Dec: 8 days with seizures Jan: 9 days, Feb: 8 days with seizures Mar: 2 days.
